content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 11, 1987

Assemblywoman Jacqueline Speier
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Our File No. I-87-052

Dear Assemblywoman Speier:

Enclosed are the materials we discussed during our meeting
last week. These include:

1. A Notice of Termination (Form 415) which you should use
if you wish to terminate the committee formed to support your
local office. Also enclosed are the "Information Manual on
Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act" and
the campaign disclosure statement (Form 490) for use in 1987.

2. An opinion issued to Merle J. Goddard, 4 FPPC Opinions 1
(No. 77-004, Jan. 11, 1978) with regard to luncheon meetings held
by the Institute of Governmental Advocates.

3. An opinion issued to Senator John A. Nejedly, 2 FPPC
Opinions 45 (No. 75-190, April 8, 1976), and an advice letter
issued to Senator Diane Watson, No. A-83-158 (Aug. 2, 1983).
These discuss solicitation by members of the legislature of funds
for non-profit and non-political organizations.

In addition, this will confirm my advice that honoraria
are treated in the same manner as gifts under the Political Reform
Act.1/ That is, if they are not used and, within 30 days of
receipt are delivered to a charitable organization without being
claimed as a charitable contribution for tax purposes, they are
not reportable on your statement of economic interests (Form 721).
(Section 82028.) However, if you deposit honoraria into a non-
profit fund (e.g., a scholarship fund), which you establish,
direct or control, they are reportable.

_______________________
1/Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Assemblywoman Jacqueline Speier
Page 2


	I hope this adequately answers all of your questions.
Please do not hesitate to contact me if I can be of further
assistance.

				Sincerely,

				[signature]
				Carla J. Wardlow
				Political Reform Consultant

sections.conclusion: Honoraria are treated in the same manner as gifts under the Political Reform Act. If they are not used and within 30 days of receipt are delivered to a charitable organization without being claimed as a charitable contribution for tax purposes, they are not reportable on Form 721. However, if honoraria are deposited into a non-profit fund that the recipient establishes, directs or controls, they are reportable.
sections.question_synthetic: How should honoraria be treated under the Political Reform Act, and what disclosure requirements apply to them?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Honoraria qualify as gifts under the Political Reform Act and are reportable on Form 721 only if deposited into a non-profit fund controlled by the recipient; otherwise, they may be donated to charity within 30 days without reporting requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION section headers present; advice is embedded in letter body. Honoraria guidance appears to be the primary substantive advice. Document also transmits forms and references prior opinions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should honoraria be treated under the Political Reform Act, and what disclosure requirements apply to them?

CONCLUSION: Honoraria are treated in the same manner as gifts under the Political Reform Act. If they are not used and within 30 days of receipt are delivered to a charitable organization without being claimed as a charitable contribution for tax purposes, they are not reportable on Form 721. However, if honoraria are deposited into a non-profit fund that the recipient establishes, directs or controls, they are reportable.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter to Assemblywoman Jacqueline Speier providing guidance on honoraria treatment under the Political Reform Act, enclosing relevant forms and prior opinions regarding campaign disclosure and solicitation by legislators.
